distractors.C: Managers carry out and operationalise policy; they do not formulate it at the company level.
distractors.D: Shareholders own the company and elect directors, but they do not write company policy themselves.

questions.stem:
Which of the following are advantages of a sole trader?
I. Shared profits
II. Ease of set up
III. Flexible working hours
questions.explanation: A sole trader keeps all the profits — they are not shared. The genuine advantages are easy set-up (few formalities) and flexible working hours, since the owner is in charge of their own time.

distractors.A: I is wrong: a sole trader keeps all profits rather than sharing them.
distractors.B: Same problem — profits are not shared in a sole-trader business.
distractors.D: Sharing profits is a feature of partnerships, not sole traders.

questions.stem: A major function of the public sector is to

options.C: serve the interests of the shareholder

questions.explanation: The public sector exists to provide goods and services for the whole society — health care, education, security and so on — rather than to chase profit. Public utilities like WASA in Trinidad and the NWC in Jamaica are good examples.

distractors.A: Profit-making is the goal of private-sector firms, not government.
distractors.C: Public sector entities serve citizens at large; they do not have shareholders to answer to.
distractors.D: Inspiring competition is more a regulatory aim than a core service-delivery function.

questions.stem: A laissez-faire leader can be described as one who

options.A: closely controls the activities of subordinates
options.B: makes decisions regardless of what subordinates may think
options.C: listens to subordinates and takes a reasonable view of the majority
options.D: stays in the background and allows subordinates to make their own decisions

questions.explanation: The phrase laissez-faire literally means 'let them do' — this leader hangs back and gives subordinates wide freedom to set their own course.

distractors.A: Closely controlling activities describes an autocratic leader.
distractors.B: Ignoring others' views is autocratic, not laissez-faire.
distractors.C: Listening and going with the majority view describes a democratic leader.

questions.stem: The diagram shows an organisation chart with X positioned above the CEO, who oversees Sales, Production and Finance Managers (with HR Supervisor and Purchasing Supervisor reporting below). Which of the following positions would be BEST suited at X?
questions.explanation: The board of directors sits above the CEO at the very top of an organisation. The CEO reports to the board on the running of the company.

distractors.A: A chief advisor offers guidance but does not have authority over the CEO.
distractors.C: The managing director is usually the same person as (or reports directly under) the CEO — not above.
distractors.D: The human resource manager is a functional manager and would slot in at the same level as Sales/Production/Finance.

diagram.alt: Organisation chart showing position X above Sales Manager, Production Manager, and Finance Manager — with HR Supervisor and Purchasing Supervisor reporting up.

questions.stem: The MAIN purpose of installing a suggestion box in a company is to get workers' views on
questions.explanation: A suggestion box is an internal communication tool — it lets workers share ideas about improving processes, safety and the workplace generally.

distractors.A: Suggestion boxes are not used to gather opinions about co-workers — that would be inappropriate.
distractors.B: Workers usually have no inside information about other companies to share this way.
distractors.D: National economic conditions are well outside the scope of an in-house suggestion box.

questions.stem: Selecting the objectives and the procedures for achieving goals is referred to as
questions.explanation: Planning is the management function of setting objectives and deciding in advance how those objectives will be achieved.

distractors.B: Organising is about arranging resources and people once the plan is in place.
distractors.C: Controlling is comparing actual performance against the plan and making corrections.
distractors.D: Motivating is encouraging workers to give their best effort — it does not by itself involve setting objectives.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements BEST describes the function of a trade union?

options.A: Securing better wages and improved working conditions for its members
options.B: Calling strikes in order to gain wage increases for its members
options.C: Representing its members at the highest political level
options.D: Improving the standard of living of its members

questions.explanation: The core function of a trade union is to negotiate on behalf of members for better pay and improved working conditions through collective bargaining.

distractors.B: Strikes are a tactic of last resort, not the union's overall function.
distractors.C: Trade unions are workplace bodies, not political representatives.
distractors.D: Improving the standard of living can be a result, but the immediate function is wages and conditions.

questions.stem: The person responsible for bringing together and organizing the factors of production is the
questions.explanation: The entrepreneur takes the risk of combining land, labour and capital to produce goods or services in pursuit of profit.

distractors.A: A trade unionist represents workers in industrial relations — not a factor-organiser.
distractors.C: Managers operate businesses on a day-to-day basis but do not necessarily own the venture or assemble the factors from scratch.
distractors.D: A financier supplies capital but does not organise all four factors of production.

questions.stem: The business opportunity element of the business plan gives a description of the

options.C: goods and services to be offered
options.D: strengths and weaknesses of the business

questions.explanation: The business opportunity section of a business plan analyses the firm's strengths and weaknesses against market opportunities and threats — essentially a SWOT-style assessment of why this is a real opportunity.

distractors.A: Potential customers fall under the marketing or target-market section.
distractors.B: Viability is established earlier in the feasibility study.
distractors.C: Goods and services to be offered are described under the products/services section.

questions.stem: The monthly payments made to an insurance company by its policyholders are known as
questions.explanation: Premiums are the regular payments policyholders make so that the insurance company will cover them against risk.

distractors.A: Deposits are sums paid into a bank account, not into an insurance policy.
distractors.C: Instalments are part-payments on a debt or hire-purchase agreement, not insurance.
distractors.D: An endowment is a type of insurance product that pays out a lump sum, not the periodic payment itself.

questions.stem: Melissa, a teenager, offered to wash cars for $10. Miss Hinds brought her car to be washed; Melissa washed it and Miss Hinds paid her $15. What type of contract is demonstrated?
questions.explanation: This is a simple contract — an oral or informal agreement supported by consideration ($15) between two parties for ordinary everyday work.

distractors.A: Social contracts are non-legal arrangements (like agreeing to meet a friend) — money was exchanged here, so it is legal.
distractors.C: 'Business contract' is not a recognised contract category in POB.
distractors.D: Specialty contracts must be in writing, signed, sealed and delivered — far more formal than this car wash deal.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements describes a specialty contract?

options.A: The contract can be made orally.
options.B: The contract need not be witnessed in writing.
options.C: The contract must be signed, sealed and delivered.
options.D: The contract needs consideration to be enforceable.

questions.explanation: A specialty contract is a formal written contract that must be signed, sealed and delivered — examples include deeds of conveyance for property and certain trust documents.

distractors.A: Oral form is a feature of simple contracts, not specialty ones.
distractors.B: Specialty contracts must be in writing and witnessed.
distractors.D: Consideration is required for simple contracts — specialty contracts are enforceable on the strength of their formal execution.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a legitimate way of terminating a contract?
questions.explanation: A simple change of mind by one party does not legally end a contract — once a contract is in force the parties are bound to it.

distractors.A: Breach (failing to honour the contract) does end it, with legal consequences.
distractors.B: Performance (both sides doing what they promised) is the natural way contracts end.
distractors.C: Lapse of time means a fixed-term contract has come to its agreed end.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of extractive production?
questions.explanation: Extractive production involves taking resources directly from the land, sea or earth — agriculture, fishing, mining and forestry are textbook examples.

distractors.A: Tourism is a service industry, not extraction of natural resources.
distractors.C: Manufacturing transforms raw materials into finished goods — that is secondary, not extractive.
distractors.D: Communication is a tertiary (service) activity.

questions.stem: Productivity can be measured by
questions.explanation: Productivity is output divided by input — it tells us how much we got out for each unit of resource we put in.

distractors.B: Inputs over outputs is the inverse and would not give a productivity ratio.
distractors.C: Inputs over revenue is not a recognised productivity formula.
distractors.D: Revenue over outputs gives an average price, not productivity.

questions.stem: Which of the following activities is MOST likely undertaken in a cottage industry?
questions.explanation: A cottage industry is small-scale production from the home using mostly local raw materials. Making sugar cakes — a popular Caribbean confection — fits perfectly.

distractors.A: Boat building usually requires a yard, heavy tools and a team of workers.
distractors.B: Bottling soft drinks needs a factory line and bottling equipment.
distractors.D: Computer repair is a service activity that needs specialised technical skills, not a typical cottage activity.

questions.stem: Which of the following conditions would constitute a linkage between two industries?

options.B: One industry shares the use of machinery with another industry.
options.C: Two industries are responsible for the manufacture of different kinds of goods.
options.D: The output of one industry of the economy becomes the input of another industry.

questions.explanation: An industrial linkage exists when one industry's output feeds another's input — for example, sugar cane farming supplies sugar mills, which supply rum distilleries.

distractors.A: Manufacturing similar goods is competition, not linkage.
distractors.B: Sharing machinery is cooperation but not the textbook definition of a linkage.
distractors.C: Producing different goods alone has nothing to do with whether the industries are linked.

questions.stem: In the Caribbean, many people are hired for sugar cane farming and very little equipment is used. This industry can BEST be described as
questions.explanation: An industry that relies more on people than machinery is labour intensive. Caribbean sugar cane cutting historically uses many workers — in Jamaica and Guyana especially — and relatively little heavy equipment.

distractors.B: Capital intensive industries (like oil refining) rely on machinery — the opposite of this scenario.
distractors.C: While the capital input may be low, the question asks for the BEST overall description, which is 'labour intensive'.
distractors.D: High capital input clearly does not fit a description that says very little equipment is used.

questions.stem: Which of the following are examples of fixed capital?
questions.explanation: Fixed capital consists of long-lasting assets used in production — buildings, plant, machinery, vehicles. They are not consumed in a single production cycle.

distractors.A: Cash balances are part of working capital, used for day-to-day operations.
distractors.B: Bank balances are also working/liquid capital, not fixed.
distractors.D: Stocks of raw materials are working capital — they get used up as production runs.

questions.stem: The producers of Power Drink created an advertisement which promoted their drink as being superior to Energy Plus Drink, produced by another business. This type of advertisement is referred to as
questions.explanation: Competitive advertising directly compares one brand to a rival to show that the advertised brand is better. Naming Energy Plus by name makes this clearly competitive.

distractors.A: Collective advertising is when several producers in the same industry advertise jointly, e.g. 'Drink Caribbean Milk'.
distractors.B: Persuasive ads use emotional appeals to influence buyers but do not necessarily compare to a named rival.
distractors.C: Informative ads provide product facts without attacking a competitor.

questions.stem: "A business should utilize channels such as wholesale, retail or internet, to make products available to consumers." Which of the following activities is BEST described in the statement above?
questions.explanation: Distribution is the marketing function that gets products from producer to consumer — wholesale, retail and online channels are all distribution channels.

distractors.B: Advertising informs and persuades consumers but does not move physical goods.
distractors.C: Market research collects information about customers and the market — it doesn't transport goods.
distractors.D: Sales promotion uses short-term incentives to boost sales but is not the broader channel-management activity.

questions.stem:
Which of the following are functions of packaging?
I. Protects the product
II. Preserves the product
III. Promotes the product
questions.explanation: Packaging serves all three roles: it protects items in transit, preserves them (especially food), and uses colour and design to promote the brand on the shelf.

distractors.A: Excludes promotion — but eye-catching packaging on a supermarket shelf is a major sales tool.
distractors.B: Excludes preservation — yet wrapping helps keep food fresh longer.
distractors.C: Excludes protection — protection from damage is one of the most basic packaging functions.

questions.stem: A situation where a business gains editorial space without having to pay for it is called
questions.explanation: Publicity is unpaid media coverage — a news article or social media post by a third party that mentions the business. Unlike advertising, the business does not pay for the space.

distractors.B: Marketing is the broad set of activities that link products to customers — much wider than free coverage.
distractors.C: Advertising is paid space and time in media, the opposite of unpaid publicity.
distractors.D: Discounting is a price-reduction promotional tool, unrelated to media coverage.

questions.stem: Product, price, promotion and place are components of a
questions.explanation: The four Ps — product, price, promotion and place — are the elements of the marketing mix, the toolkit a business adjusts to reach its target market.

distractors.A: A market study analyses customer or competitor data — it is not made up of the 4 Ps.
distractors.C: Market research is the activity of collecting market information.
distractors.D: Sales promotion is one element of the 'promotion' P, not the whole mix.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a major function of a retailer?

options.C: Providing a local supply of goods
options.D: Transporting goods to suitable storage facilities

questions.explanation: Transporting goods to storage facilities is normally a wholesaler's or logistics provider's job — retailers usually receive goods at their store ready for the consumer.

distractors.A: Retailers do prepare goods for resale (pricing, displaying, breaking bulk).
distractors.B: Selling in small quantities is the retailer's defining feature.
distractors.C: Providing a local supply (a corner shop, supermarket, pharmacy) is exactly what retailers do.

questions.stem: Which of the following documents, issued by a carrier or its agent, shows ownership of imported goods?
questions.explanation: A bill of lading is issued by the shipping carrier and acts as a receipt for goods on board, a contract of carriage, and proof of ownership of the imported goods.

distractors.B: An import licence is government authorisation to import certain goods — it is not proof of ownership.
distractors.C: A letter of credit is a banking document that guarantees payment, not ownership.
distractors.D: A bill of exchange is a written order for payment, used in trade finance.

questions.stem: Which of the following would be considered a producer?

options.A: A carpenter who stocks furniture for resale
options.B: A woman who directs films and music videos
options.C: An accountant who does payroll for large companies
options.D: A soft drink manufacturer who sells his products on the local market

questions.explanation: A producer physically makes goods. A soft drink manufacturer turns syrup, water and CO₂ into finished beverages — that is production in the strict sense.

distractors.A: A carpenter who only stocks (buys and resells) furniture is acting as a retailer, not a producer.
distractors.B: Directing films is a service activity — although it 'produces' content, in POB terms producers are usually goods-makers; the question keys on the soft drink option as the clearest producer of physical goods.
distractors.C: An accountant supplies a service — payroll processing — and is not a producer of goods.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a source of income for an individual who engages in personal budgeting?
questions.explanation: A loan repayment is money flowing OUT of your wallet to the lender — it is an expense, not income.

distractors.A: Salaries are a primary source of personal income.
distractors.C: Interest from savings adds to income.
distractors.D: Dividends paid on shares are investment income.

questions.stem: Which of the following services is provided by commercial banks?
questions.explanation: Commercial banks accept deposits from the public and lend that money out — this is their core business. Republic Bank and CIBC FirstCaribbean are everyday Caribbean examples.

distractors.A: Only the central bank issues (prints) currency.
distractors.B: Selling stocks and bonds is the work of stockbrokers and merchant banks, not commercial banks.
distractors.C: Controlling the money supply is a central bank function, not a commercial bank service.

questions.stem: One of the MAJOR functions of a central bank is to
questions.explanation: The central bank has the sole authority to issue notes and coins. The Central Bank of Trinidad and Tobago and the Bank of Jamaica are the issuers of legal tender in their respective countries.

distractors.B: Stocks and shares are issued by companies, not central banks.
distractors.C: Accepting deposits from the public is a commercial bank function.
distractors.D: Lending to the public is a commercial bank service; central banks lend mainly to commercial banks and governments.

questions.stem: Mrs Jones has several bills of fixed amounts to pay each month. She made an arrangement with her bank to make these monthly payments from her account. This service, provided by the bank, is referred to as
questions.explanation: A standing order is an instruction to a bank to pay a fixed sum to a named recipient on a regular schedule — perfect for monthly bills like rent or insurance.

distractors.A: An overdraft is short-term borrowing on a current account, not an automatic payment service.
distractors.B: A fixed deposit locks your money away for a set period to earn interest — it does not pay bills.
distractors.D: A letter of credit is a trade-finance document used in international transactions.

questions.stem: Which of the following entities can have their shares traded on the stock exchange?
questions.explanation: Only public companies (public limited companies) can list shares on the stock exchange — for example, those traded on the Trinidad and Tobago Stock Exchange or the Jamaica Stock Exchange.

distractors.A: Partnerships do not issue shares — partners contribute capital directly.
distractors.C: Private companies cannot offer shares to the general public, by definition.
distractors.D: Cooperatives use member shares but these are not freely traded on the stock market.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a function of a chamber of commerce?

options.C: Assisting in securing markets for products
options.D: Providing financial aid to hurricane victims

questions.explanation: A chamber of commerce represents local businesses and helps members find markets at home and abroad — for example, by sponsoring trade missions or trade fairs.

distractors.A: Creating jobs is the result of business activity in general, not a specific chamber function.
distractors.B: Environmental protection is mainly a role of government and dedicated NGOs.
distractors.D: Disaster relief comes from agencies like the Red Cross or ODPM, not chambers of commerce.

questions.stem:
Which of the following are examples of direct taxation?
I. Income tax
II. Purchase tax
III. Corporation tax
questions.explanation: Direct taxes are charged on income or profit and paid directly by the person or business — income tax (on individuals) and corporation tax (on companies) are textbook examples.

distractors.A: Purchase tax is an indirect tax (paid through a retailer at point of sale), so II is wrong.
distractors.B: Excludes income tax — but income tax is the most common direct tax of all.
distractors.D: Includes purchase tax, which is indirect.

questions.stem: Governments can positively influence business activity by reducing
questions.explanation: Cutting taxes leaves businesses with more after-tax profit to reinvest, hire workers and expand — a direct positive boost to business activity.

distractors.B: Reducing grants takes money away from businesses — that hurts them.
distractors.C: Reducing subsidies raises business costs and prices — also negative.
distractors.D: Cutting government expenditure can reduce demand for businesses' goods and services.

questions.stem: Governments offer subsidies to businesses to

options.A: force them to pay corporation tax
options.D: increase the prices of imported goods

questions.explanation: Subsidies are payments from government to businesses that lower production costs — the savings can be passed on as lower consumer prices, as we see with subsidised flour in some Caribbean countries.

distractors.A: Subsidies are not a tool to enforce tax payment.
distractors.C: Increasing costs is the opposite effect — that would defeat the purpose of a subsidy.
distractors.D: Subsidies on local production typically lower domestic prices, not raise import prices.

questions.stem: A 'price control' may be defined as a

options.C: grant given to producers to reduce their prices
options.D: government limit on the price of goods or services

questions.explanation: A price control is a government-imposed maximum (or minimum) price on a good or service — used to keep essential items affordable, such as price ceilings on basic foods.

distractors.A: A tax adds to prices but is not in itself a control on the price.
distractors.B: An import ban restricts goods from entering the country — that is a quota or embargo, not a price cap.
distractors.C: Grants reduce producer costs but the producer still sets the price — not a direct control.

questions.stem: The gross national product (GNP) of a country is defined as the total

options.A: value of goods and services available in the country in a given period
options.B: income earned in the country less income payable outside the country
options.C: income earned in the country in a given period less capital consumption
options.D: value of goods and services produced by the country in a given period

questions.explanation: GNP is the total value of goods and services produced by a country's nationals over a given period — including production by nationals working overseas.

distractors.A: Goods 'available' in the country includes imports — that is not what GNP measures.
distractors.B: That description is closer to net national income or net factor income from abroad.
distractors.C: Income less capital consumption gives net national product, not gross.

questions.stem: Which of the following practices would contribute to the economic growth and development of a country?

options.B: Decreased use of the country's natural resources
options.C: Exporting skilled personnel to developed countries
options.D: Provision of loans and technical assistance for establishing local businesses

questions.explanation: Helping local entrepreneurs with loans and technical assistance raises domestic production, creates jobs and builds skills — all drivers of economic growth and development.

distractors.A: Heavy importation of consumer goods drains foreign exchange and discourages local production.
distractors.B: Decreased use of natural resources by itself reduces output, not growth.
distractors.C: Exporting skilled people (the 'brain drain') deprives the country of needed skills.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a benefit of the CSME?
questions.explanation: The CARICOM Single Market and Economy aims to harmonise the region's external trade policy so member states act together when negotiating with countries outside CARICOM.

distractors.B: Housing costs are driven by national land and labour markets — the CSME does not target them.
distractors.C: Air travel costs depend mainly on fuel, taxes and airline competition — not directly tackled by CSME.
distractors.D: Equitable income distribution is largely a national policy issue, not a regional integration aim.

questions.stem: Which of the following, when increased, would NOT contribute to the improved standard of living in a country?
questions.explanation: A rising population divides the same national output among more people — without matching growth in income, this lowers per-capita standard of living.

distractors.A: More capital goods (machines, factories) raises productive capacity and improves living standards.
distractors.B: More disposable income means people can buy more goods and services.
distractors.D: Investment in technology drives productivity and higher real incomes.

questions.stem: Unemployment caused by a downturn in the economy is referred to as
questions.explanation: Cyclical unemployment rises when the economy is in recession and falls when it recovers — it tracks the business cycle.

distractors.B: Seasonal unemployment occurs when demand for labour varies with the season — like cane cutters or hotel staff in the off-season.
distractors.C: Frictional unemployment is short-term joblessness as people move between jobs.
distractors.D: Structural unemployment occurs when workers' skills no longer match available jobs.

questions.stem: Private enterprise is run MAINLY to
questions.explanation: The private sector exists primarily to make profit — that is the central motive that drives owners to take risks and put in capital.

distractors.B: Controlling inflation is a government (macroeconomic) goal.
distractors.C: Private firms create jobs as a side effect, but profit is the main aim.
distractors.D: Fair distribution of goods is a social/government concern, not a profit-driven priority.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a function of money?
questions.explanation: Indivisibility is the OPPOSITE of a useful money trait — money has to be divisible (into cents, dollars, larger notes) to make transactions of every size possible.

distractors.A: Money lets people exchange goods without bartering — that is the medium-of-exchange function.
distractors.B: Money provides a common unit so we can compare values — measure of value.
distractors.C: Money holds purchasing power for the future, as long as inflation is low — store of value.

questions.stem: Which of the following forms of payment is considered legal tender?
questions.explanation: Bank notes issued by the central bank are the official legal tender — by law, sellers must accept them as payment for debts.

distractors.A: Cheques are payment instructions and can be refused — the recipient is not legally obliged to accept one.
distractors.C: Postal orders are sold by the post office for sending money but are not legal tender.
distractors.D: Money orders are a similar payment instrument, not legal tender.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is NOT true?

options.A: Public sector refers to government-owned businesses.
options.B: Public sector businesses are not required to pay taxes.
options.C: Private sector businesses are owned by individuals.
options.D: Private sector as well as public sector businesses provide employment.

questions.explanation: Public sector entities ARE generally required to pay applicable taxes just like any other business — many state-owned enterprises file corporate tax and remit VAT/NIS for staff. Saying they are not required to pay tax is incorrect.

distractors.A: True — the public sector is government-owned business, so this is correct.
distractors.C: True — private sector businesses are owned by individuals or shareholders.
distractors.D: True — both sectors are major employers.

questions.stem: A postdated cheque is one which

options.A: is dated for payment at a future date
options.B: was written no longer than six months ago
options.C: is dated to agree with the date of the document requesting payment
options.D: is dated by the bank on the day the cheque is presented for payment

questions.explanation: A postdated cheque carries a future date — the bank will not honour it until that date arrives.

distractors.B: A cheque written within six months and not yet stale is just current, not postdated.
distractors.C: Matching the date on another document is not what 'postdated' means.
distractors.D: The bank does not redate cheques on presentation — the drawer sets the date.

questions.stem: The MAIN objective of a trade union is to encourage the

options.C: advancement of the welfare of its members
options.D: firing of workers whenever they have problems at work

questions.explanation: The overarching aim of a trade union is to advance the welfare of its members — covering pay, conditions, training, safety, and dispute resolution.

distractors.A: Recruiting new members is a means, not the main goal.
distractors.B: Higher pay is one part of welfare but not the entire mission.
distractors.D: Unions defend workers from unfair dismissal — they do not encourage firing.

questions.stem: Selecting the objectives and procedures for achieving goals is referred to as
questions.explanation: Planning is the management function of setting objectives and choosing the procedures to reach them. It comes first — before organising, leading and controlling.

distractors.A: Motivating is encouraging the team to work — it does not by itself set objectives.
distractors.B: Controlling is monitoring performance against the plan and taking corrective action.
distractors.C: Organising is arranging resources and people to carry out the plan.

questions.stem: In which of the following categories would subsidized meals for employees fall?
questions.explanation: Fringe benefits are non-cash perks offered on top of wages — subsidised meals, health insurance, car allowances, gym memberships and the like.

distractors.B: Wages and salaries are direct cash payments for work, not perks.
distractors.C: Duty-free allowances are import concessions, not employee meal subsidies.
distractors.D: Workers' compensation refers to payments for work-related injury, not daily lunches.

questions.stem: Mrs Lloyd, the manager of LM Ltd, holds regular staff meetings to listen to ideas from employees before making decisions that will affect the future of the company. What leadership style does Mrs Lloyd employ?
questions.explanation: Inviting employees' input before making decisions is the hallmark of a democratic leader — they consult the team and seek consensus.

distractors.A: An autocratic leader would decide alone without consulting staff.
distractors.C: A charismatic leader inspires through personality rather than through consultation.
distractors.D: A laissez-faire leader hands the decisions over to the team rather than holding meetings to gather input.

questions.stem: If employees are dissatisfied with working conditions, the trade union representative's FIRST action should be to

options.C: advise employees to adopt go-slow tactics
options.D: request a meeting with the board of directors

questions.explanation: The first step is always collective bargaining — start by talking to human resources and try to resolve the issue through dialogue before any industrial action.

distractors.A: Strike action is a last resort, never the first step.
distractors.C: Go-slow tactics are also industrial action — premature as a first step.
distractors.D: Going straight to the board skips the level where the matter is normally handled (HR).

questions.stem: One way in which an entrepreneur contributes to economic development is by

options.D: encouraging trade unions to represent workers

questions.explanation: By starting and growing a business, the entrepreneur creates jobs — and employment is one of the most direct contributions to economic development.

distractors.B: Importing goods drains foreign exchange and competes with local production — not a development contribution.
distractors.C: Worker incentives raise productivity but are an internal management matter.
distractors.D: Trade union activity is a separate workplace issue, not the entrepreneur's main developmental contribution.

questions.stem: In which of the following situations is collateral MOST needed?

options.D: Ordering goods from a foreign manufacturer

questions.explanation: Collateral is security for a loan — it is required precisely when borrowing from a bank or other financial institution so the lender has recourse if the borrower defaults.

distractors.A: Buying shares involves your own funds, not borrowed money typically.
distractors.B: Insurance premiums are paid for cover; no collateral is needed.
distractors.D: Foreign trade uses payment instruments like letters of credit, not collateral in the normal sense.

questions.stem: An entrepreneur who wants to maintain control of a business would be advised to establish the business as a
questions.explanation: A sole trader owns and runs the business alone — full control over every decision and all the profit.

distractors.B: Partners share both decisions and profits, so individual control is reduced.
distractors.C: A limited company has shareholders and a board, diluting any one person's control.
distractors.D: Even a private limited company requires shareholders and directors — less personal control than sole trader.

questions.stem: Which of the following elements would NOT be included in a feasibility study?
questions.explanation: A feasibility study tests whether a business idea is workable in a financial and operational sense — looking at profitability, market demand and target market. Ethical issues, while important, are not a standard element of a feasibility study.

distractors.A: Profitability is core — without expected profit the idea fails the feasibility test.
distractors.C: Identifying the target market is a standard feasibility check.
distractors.D: Market demand is essential — no demand, no business.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an element of a business plan?
questions.explanation: The standard business plan contains marketing, production, financial and human resource plans. There is no separate 'legal plan' as a standard heading — legal matters are covered within the regulatory or risk sections.

distractors.B: The financial plan is a key element — it shows projected costs and revenues.
distractors.C: The marketing plan covers product, price, place and promotion.
distractors.D: The production plan describes how the goods or services will be produced.

questions.stem: A 'purchase order' is a
questions.explanation: A purchase order is the document a buyer sends to a supplier offering to buy specified goods at agreed terms — it forms part of the contract once accepted.

distractors.A: A repeat order is one type of purchase order, but the question asks for the general definition.
distractors.B: Goods received is recorded on a goods received note, not a purchase order.
distractors.C: Outstanding debts are listed on a statement of account.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements represents a contract?

options.A: Mr Adams places a 'for sale' sign on his bike.
options.B: Seven-year-old Kevin sells his bike to Mr Adams.
options.C: Mr Adams sells a bike to seven-year-old Kevin.
options.D: Seven-year-old Kevin's mother buys a bike from Mr Adams.

questions.explanation: For a valid contract, both parties must have legal capacity. A seven-year-old is a minor and cannot enter a binding contract — but the mother (an adult) can. Only D shows two parties of full legal capacity.

distractors.A: A 'for sale' sign is just an invitation to treat — not yet a contract.
distractors.B: A minor selling a bike does not form a binding contract.
distractors.C: A minor buying a bike likewise lacks contractual capacity.

questions.stem: An essential feature of a valid contract is that

options.A: the offer has been made to the offeree
options.B: the offer is made in writing by the offeror
options.C: a counteroffer has been made by the offeree to the offeror
options.D: the offer has been accepted by the offeree either in writing or orally

questions.explanation: For a contract to exist there must be both offer and acceptance. The acceptance can usually be in writing or orally — only the agreement matters, not always the form.

distractors.A: Just making an offer is one half — without acceptance there is no contract.
distractors.B: A contract does not have to be in writing to be valid (specialty contracts excepted).
distractors.C: A counteroffer rejects the original offer and starts a new negotiation — not the essence of contract.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of cottage industries?
questions.explanation: Cottage industries are usually labour-intensive rather than automated — handmade jewellery, sewing and craft work depend on skilled hands, not machinery.

distractors.A: Cottage industries are by definition small-scale.
distractors.B: They typically operate from the home.
distractors.D: They rely heavily on local raw materials available in the community.

questions.stem: The location of an extractive industry is LEAST affected by

options.A: its proximity to large shopping areas
options.B: the presence of good roads and drainage
options.C: its proximity to sources of raw materials
options.D: the availability of an abundant labour supply

questions.explanation: Extractive industries like mining or quarrying must be near the raw materials, the workforce, and adequate roads to move output — but proximity to shopping areas is irrelevant to extraction.

distractors.B: Roads and drainage are essential to bring in equipment and move out heavy material.
distractors.C: Extractive industries must be at or near the raw material source.
distractors.D: Labour supply matters — workers must be able to reach the site.

questions.stem: The amount of labour available for productive activity is known as the
questions.explanation: The labour force is the total number of people who are working or actively seeking work — the pool from which firms can draw productive workers.

distractors.B: The 'labour movement' refers to organised trade union activity, not the workforce size.
distractors.C: The employment level counts only those currently working, not those seeking work.
distractors.D: Employment opportunities are jobs that are available — a different concept.

questions.stem: Subsistence production is BEST described as producing goods for
questions.explanation: Subsistence production means producing only enough for one's own family or community use — there is no surplus for sale or trade.

distractors.A: Producing for profit is commercial production, not subsistence.
distractors.B: Export production is large-scale commercial activity.
distractors.C: Producing for local trade still aims to sell — that is commercial, not subsistence.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a MAJOR function of consumer organizations?

options.B: To provide goods and services for consumers
options.C: To set price controls for certain commodities
options.D: To educate consumers about their rights and responsibilities

questions.explanation: Consumer organisations exist to inform and protect buyers — educating them on their rights, lobbying on standards, and providing advice on complaints.

distractors.A: Price inspection is a government regulator's job.
distractors.B: Consumer bodies do not produce or supply goods themselves.
distractors.C: Setting price controls is a government function, not a consumer organisation function.

questions.stem: Which of the following gives identity to a specific producer's goods?
questions.explanation: A brand — with its name, logo and look — gives a producer's goods a distinct identity that customers recognise and trust. Think Solo, Coca-Cola or Grace.

distractors.A: A label gives information about a product but not necessarily a unique identity.
distractors.C: A patent protects an invention or process, not the visual identity.
distractors.D: Copyright protects creative works like books and music.

questions.stem: In which of the following types of trade is the hire purchase method of buying MOST likely to be used?
questions.explanation: Hire purchase is mostly seen in retail — appliance and furniture stores let consumers take goods home and pay over time, with the store retaining ownership until the last instalment is paid.

distractors.B: Import trade uses commercial credit terms and letters of credit, not hire purchase.
distractors.C: Foreign trade uses trade-finance instruments, not consumer-style hire purchase.
distractors.D: Wholesale trade is between businesses and uses trade credit, not hire purchase.

questions.stem: Which of the following factors is LEAST likely to influence consumer behaviour?
questions.explanation: Spending patterns are the RESULT of consumer behaviour — they describe what consumers do, rather than influence it. Income, household size and the price of substitutes are the inputs that shape buying decisions.

distractors.A: Substitute prices clearly affect choice — if Pepsi is cheaper, some shoppers switch from Coca-Cola.
distractors.C: Larger households buy in different quantities and patterns.
distractors.D: Income is one of the strongest drivers of consumer demand.

questions.stem: Price discrimination may BEST be defined as setting

options.A: a lower volume of goods at a higher price
options.B: a higher volume of goods at a lower price
options.C: the same price for different products
options.D: different prices for the same product

questions.explanation: Price discrimination is charging different prices to different groups of customers (or in different markets) for the same product — for example, child versus adult cinema tickets.

distractors.A: Selling fewer at a higher price is just normal pricing, not discrimination.
distractors.B: Selling more at a lower price is volume discounting, not discrimination.
distractors.C: Same price for different products has no relation to price discrimination.

questions.stem: Which of the following actions is LEAST likely to be described as good public relations for a company?

options.D: The hosting of annual sport events

questions.explanation: Public relations builds goodwill with the wider public. A trade discount to retailers is a commercial deal, not a PR activity — the public never sees it.

distractors.A: Sponsoring a steel band — a fixture of Trinidad culture — is a classic PR move that builds community goodwill.
distractors.B: Scholarships generate strong positive publicity and community impact.
distractors.D: Sport sponsorships are highly visible PR activities.

questions.stem: Which of the following documents, issued by a carrier, shows ownership of imported goods?
questions.explanation: A bill of lading is issued by the shipping carrier and serves as a receipt, contract of carriage, and document of title — it represents ownership of the goods on board.

distractors.A: A bill of exchange is an order for payment, not a document of title.
distractors.B: A letter of credit guarantees payment but does not show ownership.
distractors.C: An import licence authorises importation — it is not a carrier-issued ownership document.

questions.stem: KD's Car Dealership wants to transport car parts from Saint Lucia to Trinidad. Which of the following methods is BEST suited for transporting the car parts?
questions.explanation: Sea freight is the most cost-effective method for moving heavy or bulky goods like car parts between Caribbean islands.

distractors.A: Air freight is fast but very expensive — only worth it for urgent or high-value light items.
distractors.C: There is no rail link between Saint Lucia and Trinidad.
distractors.D: Road transport cannot cross the open sea between islands.

questions.stem:
Which of the following sequences shows the correct order of the distribution chain?
I. Wholesaler
II. Consumer
III. Manufacturer
IV. Retailer
questions.explanation: The standard distribution chain runs from manufacturer to wholesaler to retailer to consumer — III, I, IV, II.

distractors.A: Starts with the wholesaler — but goods come FROM the manufacturer.
distractors.B: Puts retailer before wholesaler — goods normally pass through the wholesaler first.
distractors.D: Ends with the wholesaler — but the consumer is always the last link in the chain.

questions.stem: An instruction given to a commercial bank to make regular payments of a fixed amount to a named person is called a
questions.explanation: A standing order instructs the bank to pay a fixed sum to the same payee on a fixed schedule — useful for rent or insurance premiums.

distractors.A: A direct debit is set up by the payee and the amount can vary — not a fixed instruction by the payer.
distractors.B: A credit transfer is a one-off payment from one account to another.
distractors.D: A letter of credit is a trade-finance document used in international transactions.

questions.stem: Tina invests her savings by buying shares in a company. From this investment, Tina expects to receive
questions.explanation: Shareholders receive dividends — a portion of company profits distributed to them based on the number of shares they hold.

distractors.A: Profits are kept by the company; only the part declared as dividend goes to shareholders.
distractors.B: Interest is paid on debt instruments like bonds, not on shares.
distractors.C: Revenues are sales income earned by the company itself, not paid to shareholders.

questions.stem: Which of the following financial records shows the assets and liabilities for John's Grocery Store?
questions.explanation: A balance sheet presents what a business owns (assets) and what it owes (liabilities) on a specific date — a snapshot of its financial position.

distractors.A: A cash book records cash receipts and payments, not the full balance.
distractors.C: The income statement shows revenue and expenses, leading to profit or loss — not assets and liabilities.
distractors.D: The cash flow statement tracks cash movements over a period, not the position of assets and liabilities.

questions.stem: Subsidies refer to
questions.explanation: Subsidies are grants from government to producers (or sometimes consumers) that lower the cost of producing or buying certain goods.

distractors.A: Tax withholdings are revenue to government, not payments out.
distractors.B: Loans must be repaid; subsidies do not.
distractors.D: Investment profits are private earnings, unrelated to government grants.

questions.stem: Governments can encourage business activity by
questions.explanation: Cutting taxes raises businesses' after-tax profit, encouraging investment, hiring and expansion.

distractors.B: Reducing grants takes money away from businesses, hurting activity.
distractors.C: Reducing government spending lowers demand for businesses' goods and services.
distractors.D: A budget surplus means government taxes more than it spends — a contractionary stance, not supportive.

questions.stem: Education contributes to economic growth by ensuring that the workforce is highly
questions.explanation: An educated workforce is more efficient — better skills, better problem solving, faster learning of new technology — all of which drive higher productivity and growth.

distractors.A: A 'structured' workforce describes its arrangement, not its productivity contribution.
distractors.B: Classification refers to grouping workers — not the productivity benefit of education.
distractors.D: Mobility is a separate trait — important for matching skills to jobs but not the direct growth driver here.

questions.stem: Inflation can BEST be described as

options.A: changes in the level of prices of goods and services
options.B: a fall in the level of prices of goods and services
options.C: a general and sustained rise in the level of prices
options.D: fluctuations in the general price level

questions.explanation: Inflation is a general and sustained rise in the average level of prices — a one-off price jump or random fluctuation does not count.

distractors.A: Just 'changes' is too general — prices change up or down all the time.
distractors.B: A fall in prices is deflation, not inflation.
distractors.D: Fluctuations could go either way — inflation specifically means a sustained rise.

questions.stem: Export licences are issued MAINLY to

options.A: encourage local businesses to produce more goods for export
options.B: enable the government to control the flow of goods being exported
options.C: indicate to the importing country that the goods are of a high standard
options.D: indicate to the importing country the price at which the goods are to be sold

questions.explanation: An export licence is a government authorisation that controls which goods may leave the country and in what quantities — used to protect strategic resources or comply with international agreements.

distractors.A: Encouragement comes through subsidies and incentives, not licences.
distractors.C: Quality is shown by certificates of origin or standards certificates, not export licences.
distractors.D: Export licences do not set prices.

questions.stem:
Which of the following factors are LIKELY to affect the standard of living of a country?
I. A high crime rate
II. A low literacy rate
III. Reduction in resources
questions.explanation: All three drag the standard of living downwards: crime makes people feel unsafe and discourages investment, low literacy limits economic opportunity, and dwindling resources reduce output.

distractors.A: Excludes resource reduction, which clearly affects living standards.
distractors.B: Excludes low literacy, which is a strong predictor of poor living standards.
distractors.C: Excludes the high crime rate — but crime is a major quality-of-life issue.

questions.stem: The introduction of technology in the production process may affect society by

options.D: providing more jobs for unskilled workers

questions.explanation: Automation often replaces routine manual tasks, so it tends to reduce the demand for unskilled labour while raising demand for technicians and engineers.

distractors.A: Pollution effects depend on the specific technology — not a guaranteed social effect.
distractors.C: Brain drain is the loss of skilled people overseas — not a direct effect of introducing technology at home.
distractors.D: Technology generally substitutes for unskilled labour rather than adding more such jobs.

questions.stem: Which of the following methods can a government use to reduce unemployment?
questions.explanation: An employment levy on firms can be designed to encourage hiring (e.g. payroll-based incentives) or fund job training — used as part of a labour-market policy. Note: this is a soft call; among the four, the others all REDUCE economic activity (deflation, higher taxes, higher interest rates) which would worsen unemployment.

distractors.A: Deflating the economy reduces demand and increases unemployment.
distractors.B: Higher income taxes reduce disposable income, lowering consumer demand and jobs.
distractors.C: Higher interest rates discourage borrowing and investment, reducing employment.

questions.stem: A MAJOR function of CARICOM is to

options.B: foster intraregional trade and common services
options.C: encourage the transfer of technology within the region
options.D: allow for the unrestricted movement of persons among the territories

questions.explanation: CARICOM's central economic mission is to foster intraregional trade and shared services among member states — building a stronger common market and economy across the region.

distractors.A: Collaboration is a means; intraregional trade is the major economic function.
distractors.C: Technology transfer is one objective among many, not the major function.
distractors.D: Free movement is a part of the CSME but applies mainly to certain categories of skilled persons, not all citizens fully.

questions.stem: Economic dualism refers to
questions.explanation: Economic dualism describes a country with two very different sectors — usually a modern, capital-intensive sector alongside a traditional, low-productivity sector — leading to uneven growth across the economy.

distractors.B: Technology advancement is a separate concept — innovation, not dualism.
distractors.C: Foreign investment can drive dualism but is not its definition.
distractors.D: Growth in agriculture alone is sectoral growth, not the two-sector divide that dualism describes.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a characteristic of the sole trader form of business which is NOT advantageous to the owner?
questions.explanation: Unlimited liability means the owner is personally responsible for all business debts — even personal assets like a home can be seized. That is the major drawback of operating as a sole trader.

distractors.B: Low operational cost is a benefit of being a sole trader — fewer legal and admin overheads.
distractors.C: Keeping all the profits is a clear advantage, not a drawback.
distractors.D: Close customer rapport is one of the strengths of small sole-trader businesses.

questions.stem: Characteristics of online communication include I. instant feedback II. detailed messaging III. documented evidence
questions.explanation: Online communication offers instant feedback (chat, email reply), supports detailed messaging with attachments, and creates documented evidence through saved logs and threads — so all three apply.

distractors.A: Detailed messaging is also a feature — emails and online docs handle long, structured content well.
distractors.B: Detailed messaging is also a feature, so 'I and III only' is incomplete.
distractors.C: Instant feedback is also a feature; messages can be answered in real time.

questions.stem: A MAJOR role of a trade union is to encourage the

options.C: advancement of the welfare of its members
options.D: sacking of workers whenever they have problems at work

questions.explanation: The core purpose of a trade union is to advance the welfare of its members — better pay, conditions, training and protection. Other actions are tools toward that overall goal.

distractors.A: Recruiting members is a means, not the major role of the union itself.
distractors.B: Pushing for higher pay and leave is one tactic, but the broader role is overall member welfare.
distractors.D: Unions defend members from unfair sacking — they would never encourage dismissals.

questions.stem: Which of the following phrases BEST describes an authoritarian leader?

options.C: Allowing workers to plan their own work
options.D: Consulting those likely to be affected by decisions

questions.explanation: An authoritarian (autocratic) leader makes decisions alone and expects them to be carried out without input from staff. The word "authoritarian" itself signals top-down control with no consultation.

distractors.A: Taking suggestions describes a democratic style, where the leader actively invites input.
distractors.C: Letting workers plan their own work describes a laissez-faire style, where the leader steps back entirely.
distractors.D: Consulting people affected by decisions is also a hallmark of democratic leadership — the opposite of authoritarian.

questions.stem: Richard has been an employee of MTSL for over ten years and is up for a promotion. A manager's nephew who was recently hired has been promoted ahead of Richard, and an upset Richard has taken the issue to his trade union. This conflict is as a result of
questions.explanation: Promoting a recently hired relative ahead of a long-serving employee is a textbook example of unfair practices in the workplace — favouritism rather than merit.

distractors.C: Fringe benefits are extras like meals or insurance, not promotion fairness.
distractors.D: Working conditions refer to the physical environment and hours, not promotion decisions.

questions.stem: In which of the following categories would subsidized meals for employees fall?
questions.explanation: Subsidised meals are an extra perk on top of pay, which makes them a classic fringe benefit — like company cars or health insurance.

distractors.B: Duty allowances are extra cash for travel or special duties, not a meal subsidy.
distractors.C: Wages and salaries are direct cash pay for work done, not a subsidised meal.
distractors.D: Workmen's compensation is a payout for injuries on the job.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of an entrepreneur?

options.B: Roger goes to the bank to get a loan.
options.C: Sally occasionally babysits her neighbour's child.
options.D: Donna operates an online bakery from her home.

questions.explanation: An entrepreneur organises the factors of production to run a business and bears the risk. Donna's online bakery is a real venture combining capital, labour and risk-taking.

distractors.B: Renting office space is just one input; it doesn't show running a business.
distractors.C: Getting a loan is a step toward starting up but not, by itself, entrepreneurship.
distractors.D: Occasional babysitting is informal, not an organised business venture.

questions.stem: The MAIN benefit of collateral to the lender is that it

options.A: can be seized if the borrower fails to pay
options.B: improves the borrower's chance of getting a loan
options.C: determines the financial position of the borrower
options.D: gives the lender an idea of the state of the business

questions.explanation: Collateral is security pledged against a loan — its main value to the lender is that the asset can be seized and sold if the borrower defaults.

distractors.B: Better loan approval is a benefit to the borrower, not the lender.
distractors.C: Collateral is an asset, not a measure of overall finances.
distractors.D: Lenders use financial statements to judge the state of the business — not collateral.

questions.stem: The condensed version of the business plan, which provides an overview of the business, is contained in the
questions.explanation: The executive summary is the short, high-impact opening section of a business plan — it gives readers an overview of the venture in a page or two.

distractors.A: The marketing plan covers product, price, promotion and place — only one part of the business.
distractors.B: A feasibility study comes before the plan, testing whether the idea can work.
distractors.C: 'Keynote summary' is not a standard term in business planning.

questions.stem: The monthly payments collected by an insurance company from its policyholders are known as
questions.explanation: The regular payments made to keep an insurance policy active are called premiums. Skip the premium and the policy can lapse.

distractors.A: Deposits are sums kept at a bank — different financial product.
distractors.C: Instalments usually refer to portions of a loan or credit purchase, not insurance.
distractors.D: An endowment is a type of life policy that pays a lump sum at the end of a term.

questions.stem: Which of the following factors prevents an agreement from being considered a valid contract?
questions.explanation: Misrepresentation — a false statement of fact that induces a party to enter the contract — makes the contract voidable. The other items are essentials that a valid contract must have.

distractors.A: Consideration (something of value exchanged) is a requirement of a valid contract.
distractors.B: A legal purpose is essential — illegal contracts are unenforceable, but having a legal purpose helps validity.
distractors.D: Offer and acceptance are the foundation of any contract.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a primary industry?
questions.explanation: Primary industries extract raw materials from nature — mining, fishing and farming all fit. Building (construction) processes materials and is a secondary industry.

distractors.A: Mining extracts raw materials directly from the earth — primary.
distractors.B: Fishing harvests directly from the sea — primary.
distractors.C: Farming produces raw food and crops directly — primary.

questions.stem: The factor of production which earns interest is
questions.explanation: Capital earns interest. The standard pairings are: land–rent, labour–wages, capital–interest, enterprise–profit.

distractors.A: Land earns rent.
distractors.B: Labour earns wages or salary.
distractors.D: Enterprise earns profit.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT classified as a natural resource?
questions.explanation: Natural resources exist in nature without human production. Sugar is processed from sugarcane — it is a manufactured good, not a natural resource. Sugarcane would be the natural resource.

distractors.B: Bauxite is mined directly from the earth — a natural resource (and big in Jamaica and Guyana).
distractors.C: Sunlight is freely supplied by nature.
distractors.D: Crude oil is extracted as it is found in the ground — natural.

questions.stem: Labour is BEST defined as the

options.A: ability to carry out management functions effectively
options.B: physical and mental capabilities used to perform tasks
options.C: level of training received by the employees of an organization
options.D: total number of persons who are employed in government ministries

questions.explanation: Labour is the human effort — both physical and mental — used to produce goods and services. It is one of the four factors of production.

distractors.A: That describes management ability, a sub-set of labour.
distractors.C: Training level describes the quality of labour, not the definition itself.
distractors.D: That defines public sector employment, not labour as a factor of production.

questions.stem: Which of the following are features of a cottage industry? I. Family assistance II. Mass production III. Minimal investment
questions.explanation: A cottage industry is small-scale, home-based production typically done with family help and minimal investment. Mass production needs factories and machinery — the opposite of cottage industry. Think of Caribbean home-based pepper-sauce or craft producers.

distractors.A: Mass production doesn't fit a cottage industry — they're small-scale.
distractors.C: Mass production doesn't fit a cottage industry.
distractors.D: Mass production rules this out.

questions.stem: Which of the following can be described as a marketing activity?

options.D: Ensuring that workers are given fringe benefits

questions.explanation: Distribution (place) is one of the four Ps of marketing — getting goods to retail outlets so customers can buy them is a core marketing activity.

distractors.A: Internal staff bulletins are HR/communication, not marketing.
distractors.C: Recruitment ads target job-seekers, which is a human-resource activity.
distractors.D: Fringe benefits are part of HR/employee relations, not marketing.

questions.stem: Which of the following activities carried out in a large firm will NOT be under the supervision of the marketing manager?
questions.explanation: Purchasing (buying raw materials and supplies) is normally handled by the procurement or production department, not marketing. Marketing covers the four Ps — including packaging, advertising and distribution.

distractors.A: Packaging is part of product presentation — marketing.
distractors.C: Advertising falls clearly under promotion in the marketing mix.
distractors.D: Distribution (place) is one of the four Ps — marketing.

questions.stem: Which of the following lists shows the correct order of the chain of distribution?
questions.explanation: The traditional chain of distribution flows from producer to user: Manufacturer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer.

distractors.A: Reversed order — the consumer is at the end, not the start.
distractors.B: Wholesaler doesn't come before the manufacturer; goods originate with the manufacturer.
distractors.C: Retailers buy from wholesalers, not the other way around.

questions.stem: Which of the following services are provided by commercial banks? I. Controlling the supply of money II. Offering loans to small businesses III. Accepting deposits from customers
questions.explanation: Commercial banks accept deposits and lend to individuals and businesses. Controlling the money supply is the job of the central bank, not commercial banks.

distractors.A: I is wrong — money supply is the central bank's role.
distractors.B: I is wrong — money supply is the central bank's role.
distractors.D: I is wrong — money supply is the central bank's role.

questions.stem: Mr Jones has several bills of fixed amounts to pay each month. His bank manager agrees to pay them monthly from Mr Jones' account. This service, provided by the bank, refers to
questions.explanation: A standing order is a customer's instruction to the bank to pay a fixed amount from their account at regular intervals — perfect for monthly bills.

distractors.A: An overdraft is permission to draw beyond the account balance, not a payment instruction.
distractors.B: Acting as trustee is managing assets on behalf of someone, not paying bills.
distractors.D: A letter of credit is a banker's guarantee used in international trade, not a domestic bill payment service.

questions.stem: Tax paid on goods entering a country is referred to as
questions.explanation: Customs duty is the tax charged at the border on imported goods — used to raise revenue and protect local industry.

distractors.A: Income tax is on personal earnings, not goods.
distractors.B: Excise duty is on specific domestic goods (like tobacco or alcohol), not imports at the border.
distractors.D: Corporation tax is on company profits, not on imports.

questions.stem: What is the MOST likely effect on a developing economy when professional and skilled labour migrate to developed countries?

options.B: Unskilled labourers may demand higher wages.
options.C: Production levels of goods and services will decrease.
options.D: The demand for skilled labour and professional services will decrease.

questions.explanation: This is the brain drain. When doctors, teachers and engineers leave the Caribbean for North America or the UK, production of goods and services falls because key skills are missing.

distractors.A: Workers' effort isn't automatically increased by others leaving.
distractors.B: Unskilled wages may not rise at all — most affected sectors need skills.
distractors.D: Demand for skilled services usually rises (becomes harder to meet), not falls.

questions.stem: Which of the following measures are used to protect the environment? I. Recycling II. Deforestation III. Water conservation
questions.explanation: Recycling and water conservation protect the environment. Deforestation destroys it — clearing forests increases erosion, flooding and CO2.

distractors.A: Deforestation harms the environment, so II must be excluded.
distractors.C: Deforestation harms the environment, so II must be excluded.
distractors.D: Deforestation harms the environment, so II must be excluded.

questions.stem: KMD Enterprise is a clothing store with four branches. The management of KMD is trying to enhance its internal communication system. Which of the following management information systems (MIS) is MOST appropriate for the buying and selling of goods?
questions.explanation: Transaction processing systems (TPS) handle the day-to-day buying and selling transactions — sales, purchases, receipts and payments — exactly what a clothing chain needs across branches.

distractors.A: Decision-support systems help managers analyse complex problems, not record routine sales.
distractors.C: Executive support systems help top management with strategic decisions, not daily transactions.
distractors.D: Office automation handles documents, email and scheduling, not goods transactions.

questions.stem: A MAJOR function of CARICOM is to

options.B: encourage the transfer of technology within the region
options.C: allow for the unrestricted movement of persons among the territories
options.D: foster intraregional trade and common services

questions.explanation: A central function of CARICOM is to foster intraregional trade and common services — through the CSME, common external tariff, and joint institutions like the CCJ and CXC.

distractors.A: Collaboration is too vague — CARICOM specifically targets trade and shared services.
distractors.B: Technology transfer happens but is not the major stated function.
distractors.C: Free movement is partial (skilled categories), not unrestricted for everyone.

questions.stem: Which of the following practices would contribute to the economic growth and development of a country?

options.B: Decreased use of the country's natural resources
options.C: Export of skilled personnel to developed countries
options.D: Provision of loans and technical assistance for establishing local businesses

questions.explanation: Providing loans and technical assistance to start and grow local businesses creates jobs, boosts output and builds skills — directly fuelling economic growth and development.

distractors.A: More imports usually drains foreign exchange and harms local producers.
distractors.B: Underusing natural resources slows growth — sustainable use is the goal.
distractors.C: Exporting skilled workers (brain drain) reduces the workforce needed for development.

questions.stem: One feature of a 'free market system' is that

options.B: the government is the major controller of decision-making
options.C: producers are allowed to supply what the consumer wants
options.D: both the government and producers are involved in decision-making

questions.explanation: In a free market system, producers decide what to make based on consumer demand — supply responds to what people want to buy, with minimal government interference.

distractors.A: Free markets actually encourage strong competition among producers, not less.
distractors.B: Government as the major decision-maker describes a command (planned) economy, not a free market.
distractors.D: Joint government and producer decision-making describes a mixed economy.

questions.stem: Which of the following types of business environments is MOST typical of the Commonwealth Caribbean?
questions.explanation: The mixed economy — where the public and private sectors operate side by side — is the typical model across the Commonwealth Caribbean. Trinidad, Jamaica and Barbados all combine state-owned utilities with private businesses.

distractors.A: A 'corporate state' is a fascist political model, not a Caribbean economic system.
distractors.C: Centrally planned (command) economies are run entirely by the state — not the Caribbean norm.
distractors.D: Perfect competition is a theoretical model rarely seen in practice anywhere.

questions.stem:
Which of the following are advantages of a sole trader?
I. Ease of setting up.
II. Profits are not shared.
III. Flexible working hours.
questions.explanation: All three statements are advantages of a sole trader. Setting up is quick and inexpensive, the owner keeps all the profits, and the owner sets his or her own working hours.

distractors.A: Excludes flexible working hours, which is also a sole trader advantage.
distractors.B: Excludes the fact that profits are not shared.
distractors.C: Excludes ease of setup, which is one of the sole trader's biggest advantages.

questions.stem: The management team of Scoot Motor has discovered that the productivity levels of workers have fallen. The team met and without consulting the workers took a decision to shorten the coffee break and lunch period. What leadership style has the management team utilized in making its decision?
questions.explanation: Making a decision without consulting workers and imposing it on them is the textbook mark of an autocratic leadership style.

distractors.B: A democratic leader would have consulted the workers before deciding.
distractors.C: Charismatic leaders inspire through personality but the question focuses on the lack of consultation, not charm.
distractors.D: Laissez-faire leaders leave workers to decide for themselves — the opposite of what happened here.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an activity in the planning process?
questions.explanation: Setting goals, establishing policies and identifying resources are all part of planning. Delegating responsibilities is part of organising — handing tasks to others — and is not a planning activity.

distractors.A: Setting goals is the starting point of any plan.
distractors.B: Establishing policies provides the framework that guides planning decisions.
distractors.C: Identifying the resources needed is a key planning step.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a form of industrial action in which trade unions are MOST likely to ask workers to participate?
questions.explanation: Work to rule is a form of industrial action where workers do exactly — and only — what their job description says, slowing operations to pressure employers. Trade unions ask members to participate in this.

distractors.B: Arbitration is a dispute-settlement process led by a neutral third party, not an action by workers.
distractors.C: Bargaining (collective bargaining) is negotiation, not industrial action.
distractors.D: Mediation is a peaceful resolution process, not a worker action.

questions.stem: The condensed version of the business plan is contained in the
questions.explanation: The executive summary is the condensed version of a business plan — a one- or two-page overview placed at the front so investors and lenders can grasp the whole proposal quickly.

distractors.B: 'Keynote summary' is not a standard component of a business plan.
distractors.C: The marketing plan is one section of the full plan, not its summary.
distractors.D: The feasibility plan/study is a separate document that tests whether an idea is workable.

questions.stem: The principle of insurance where individuals would be compensated for damages or losses incurred is referred to as
questions.explanation: The principle of indemnity means the insurer compensates the insured for actual losses suffered — restoring the policyholder to the financial position they were in before the loss, no more.

distractors.B: Proximate cause links the loss to the insured event but isn't about compensation itself.
distractors.C: Insurable interest means the policyholder must stand to lose financially if the event happens.
distractors.D: Pooling of risks describes how premiums from many policyholders cover the few who claim.

questions.stem: Which of the following are NOT linkage industries?
questions.explanation: Linkage industries are those where the output of one becomes the input of another. Fishing and farming are both primary industries that produce different outputs — they don't feed into each other, so they are not linked.

distractors.B: Caribbean agriculture supplies hotels and restaurants, linking it to tourism.
distractors.C: Sugar feeds directly into confectionery production — a classic linkage.
distractors.D: Lumber from forestry is the raw material used to build boats — a direct linkage.

questions.stem: In the Caribbean, many people are hired for sugarcane farming and very little equipment is used. This industry can BEST be described as
questions.explanation: An industry that uses more workers than machines is described as labour intensive. Caribbean sugarcane farming, with hand-cutting and minimal mechanisation, is the classic example.

distractors.B: Capital intensive industries use lots of machinery and equipment — the opposite of this scenario.
distractors.C: 'Low capital input' overlaps with labour intensive but the sharper textbook term is labour intensive.
distractors.D: High capital input describes a capital-intensive operation — not this one.

questions.stem: Productivity can be measured by
questions.explanation: Productivity is the ratio of output to input — how much you get out for what you put in. The standard formula is Outputs / Inputs.

distractors.A: Inputs / Outputs gives the wrong direction — fewer outputs per input would suggest higher productivity.
distractors.B: Inputs / Revenue is not a productivity measure.
distractors.D: Revenue / Inputs measures revenue efficiency, not productivity.

questions.stem: Labour is BEST defined as the

options.A: physical and mental capabilities used to perform tasks
options.B: ability to carry out management functions effectively
options.C: level of skills training received by individuals who are working
options.D: total number of persons who hold jobs in government ministries

questions.explanation: Labour is the human factor of production — the physical and mental effort people contribute to making goods or providing services.

distractors.B: Carrying out management functions is the role of enterprise, not labour generally.
distractors.C: Skill level affects the quality of labour but isn't the definition.
distractors.D: Government workers are a small subset, not the meaning of labour.

questions.stem: Rich Chocolates Ltd erected a tent in several supermarkets for the purpose of exhibiting all the company's products. This activity is referred to as
questions.explanation: Setting up a tent in a supermarket to display all of a company's products is a classic example of merchandising — the visual and physical presentation of goods to encourage sales.

distractors.A: Retailing is selling to the final consumer; the focus here is on display, not the sale.
distractors.B: Advertising uses media messages, not in-store product displays.
distractors.D: Public relations builds image through events and goodwill, not product exhibits.

questions.stem: Which of the following operations is an example of sales promotion?
