questions.explanation: Giving away free samples is a textbook sales promotion — a short-term incentive to encourage trial and boost sales.

distractors.A: Warehousing is storage, part of distribution — not promotion.
distractors.C: Servicing goods is after-sales service, not a promotion technique.
distractors.D: Gathering information is market research, not promotion.

questions.stem: John has sought permission to prevent the local art gallery from reproducing and selling his paintings. This legal means of protecting his work is referred to as a
questions.explanation: Copyright protects original creative works — paintings, songs, books, software — preventing others from reproducing or selling them without permission.

distractors.A: Patents protect inventions and processes, not artistic works.
distractors.B: A brand identifies a producer's goods but doesn't legally protect art.
distractors.D: A franchise is a business licensing arrangement, not artistic protection.

questions.stem: A global positioning system (GPS) provides

options.A: firms with the capability to manage their inventories and reduce expenses
options.B: satellite tracking services that can be used in commercial and personal applications
options.C: saving options to firms by increasing the efficiency of their management information systems
options.D: simulation services to help designers avoid waste by creating models to assist manufacturers in making business decisions

questions.explanation: A global positioning system (GPS) uses satellites to provide precise location and tracking, used by businesses (fleet management, deliveries) and individuals (navigation apps) alike.

distractors.A: Inventory management is a function of management information systems, not GPS.
distractors.C: MIS efficiency is a management information system feature, not GPS.
distractors.D: Design simulation is a CAD feature, not GPS.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the final step in a supply chain operation?

options.A: Transformation of natural resources into finished products
options.B: Movement to and storage of raw materials in the warehouse
options.C: Processing of raw materials and components into finished goods
options.D: Delivering the finished product from point of origin to point of destination

questions.explanation: The final step in any supply chain is delivering the finished product to the end customer — the destination point.

distractors.A: Transformation of raw materials happens earlier in the chain.
distractors.B: Storing raw materials is an early stage, not the final one.
distractors.C: Processing into finished goods is the manufacturing step, before delivery.

questions.stem: Investment can be defined as

options.A: the amount of capital owned by the government
options.B: the amount of money available at a given time
options.C: additions to the capital stock of a country
options.D: the stock of goods in a country

questions.explanation: In economics, investment means additions to a country's capital stock — new factories, machines, and infrastructure that increase productive capacity.

distractors.A: Government-owned capital is just one slice, not the definition of investment.
distractors.B: The stock of money on hand is liquidity, not investment.
distractors.D: Goods sitting in stock are inventory, not investment in the economic sense.

questions.stem: The success of a government's economic policies can be MOST effectively measured by

options.A: an increase in the level of policies
options.B: an increase in the standard of living
options.C: a decrease in the level of employment
options.D: a decrease in the volume of production

questions.explanation: The standard of living reflects how well citizens can afford goods, services and basic needs. A rising standard of living is the most effective real-world signal that economic policies are working.

distractors.A: More policies on paper doesn't prove they are succeeding.
distractors.C: A drop in employment would be a sign of failure, not success.
distractors.D: Falling production would mean the economy is shrinking.

questions.stem: In which of the following types of economic systems does the state own and control all the country's economic resources?
questions.explanation: A planned (command) economy is one in which the state owns and controls all economic resources and makes all production decisions.

distractors.A: A mixed economy combines government and private ownership — the state doesn't own everything.
distractors.C: In a capitalist economy, individuals and firms own resources, not the state.
distractors.D: A subsistence economy is small-scale and family-based, not state-controlled.

questions.stem: Which of the following phrases BEST describes an authoritarian leader?

options.C: Allowing workers to plan their own work
options.D: Consulting those likely to be affected by decisions

questions.explanation: An authoritarian (autocratic) leader makes decisions alone and tells subordinates what to do, without seeking input from the team.

distractors.A: Welcoming suggestions describes a more democratic style.
distractors.C: Letting workers plan their own work is the laissez-faire style.
distractors.D: Consulting affected staff before deciding is democratic, not authoritarian.

questions.stem: Richard has been an employee of MTSL for over ten years. He is due for a promotion at the end of the year. However, a manager's nephew who was recently hired has been promoted ahead of Richard. An upset Richard has taken this issue to his trade union. This conflict is as a result of
questions.explanation: Promoting a recently-hired relative ahead of a long-serving employee is a textbook example of unfair practices (nepotism), and it's the kind of issue trade unions step in to challenge.

distractors.B: Job security relates to the risk of losing a job, not promotion fairness.
distractors.C: Fringe benefits are extras like meals or insurance, not the issue here.
distractors.D: Working conditions concern safety and the work environment, not promotions.

questions.stem: In which of the following management functions is a manager engaged when he grants a worker 'time off with pay'?
questions.explanation: Granting paid time off is a way of rewarding and encouraging the worker — a classic motivating action by management.

distractors.A: Planning is about setting goals and procedures, not granting time off.
distractors.B: Delegating is assigning tasks to subordinates, not giving paid leave.
distractors.D: Coordinating is integrating different activities, not motivating individuals.

questions.stem: Mr John offered his car for sale for $70 000. Miss Maraj said that she would pay him $66 000 for it. Which of the following phrases BEST describes Miss Maraj's action?
questions.explanation: By offering a different price ($66 000 instead of $70 000), Miss Maraj rejected Mr John's offer and made a counter offer. Acceptance must mirror the original terms exactly.

distractors.A: Acceptance requires agreeing to the offered $70 000 — she didn't.
distractors.B: No contract was formed because the offer wasn't accepted as stated.
distractors.D: An invitation to treat invites offers; she made an offer of her own.

questions.stem: What type of capital is defined as money used for the day-to-day operations of a business?
questions.explanation: Working capital — also called circulating capital — is the money a business uses for everyday operations such as paying wages, buying stock and settling utility bills.

distractors.A: Fixed capital refers to long-life assets like buildings and machinery.
distractors.B: Floating capital is sometimes used as a synonym for working capital but is not the standard term.
distractors.C: Nominal capital is the maximum capital a company is registered to raise.

questions.stem: Which of the following outcomes is LEAST likely to result from growth in an organization?
questions.explanation: Growth usually improves productivity, division of labour and capital investment. Communication, however, tends to worsen as more layers and people are added — making it the LEAST likely positive outcome.

distractors.A: Greater productivity is one of the main benefits of growth, so it is likely.
distractors.B: Larger firms can specialise more, so division of labour increases.
distractors.D: Bigger firms can afford more equipment and infrastructure.

questions.stem: The term 'human resources' refers to

options.A: materials and goods made by human beings
options.B: capital invested by shareholders in a company
options.C: raw materials used in the manufacture of goods
options.D: persons available for productive and professional services

questions.explanation: Human resources are the people available to provide productive and professional services — the workforce of an economy or organisation.

distractors.A: Goods made by humans are output, not the human resource itself.
distractors.B: Capital from shareholders is financial capital, not human resources.
distractors.C: Raw materials are natural resources, not human ones.

questions.stem: Which of the following can be described as a marketing activity?

options.C: Issuing bulletins relating to staff matters
options.D: Ensuring that workers are given fringe benefits

questions.explanation: Distributing commodities to outlets is part of the marketing mix's place element — getting goods to where customers can buy them.

distractors.B: Advertising vacancies is a human resources activity, not marketing.
distractors.C: Internal staff bulletins are internal communication, not marketing.
distractors.D: Fringe benefits are part of compensation/HR, not marketing.

questions.stem: Which of the following elements comprise the marketing mix?
questions.explanation: The traditional marketing mix — known as the 4 Ps — is product, price, promotion and place.

distractors.A: 'Public' is not one of the 4 Ps.
distractors.C: Packaging is part of product, not a separate P in the standard mix.
distractors.D: Same issue — packaging is a sub-element of product, and price is missing here.

questions.stem: Which of the following is LEAST likely to be described as good public relations for a company?
questions.explanation: Public relations builds goodwill with the general public — sponsoring bands, scholarships and events all do that. Cutting prices to retailers is a trade discount; it benefits resellers, not the wider public.

distractors.A: Sponsoring a steel band wins community goodwill — classic PR in Trinidad.
distractors.B: Scholarships build a positive reputation in the community.
distractors.C: Hosting sports events is a textbook PR activity.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT part of the distribution chain?
questions.explanation: The distribution chain moves goods from producer to consumer: manufacturer → wholesaler → retailer → consumer. A contractor is hired for a job (e.g., construction) and is not a link in this chain.

distractors.A: The manufacturer is the starting point of the chain.
distractors.B: The wholesaler buys in bulk from the manufacturer and sells to retailers.
distractors.D: The retailer sells directly to consumers — the last link before purchase.

questions.stem: KD Cars Dealership wants to transport car parts from Saint Lucia to Trinidad. Which of the following methods is BEST suited for transporting the car parts?
questions.explanation: Heavy car parts moving between Caribbean islands such as Saint Lucia and Trinidad are best transported by sea — far cheaper than air for bulky cargo, and rail/road can't cross open water.

distractors.A: Air is fast but very expensive for heavy auto parts.
distractors.C: There is no rail link between Saint Lucia and Trinidad.
distractors.D: Road transport cannot cross the sea between islands.

questions.stem: A supply chain is BEST defined as the

options.A: storage of work-in-progress and finished goods
options.B: processing of raw materials and components into finished goods
options.C: delivery of finished products from the point of origin to the point of destination
options.D: system of organizations and resources involved in moving products from suppliers to customers

questions.explanation: A supply chain is the entire system of organisations and resources involved in moving products from suppliers all the way to customers — including manufacturers, transporters, warehouses, wholesalers and retailers.

distractors.A: Storage is just one piece of the supply chain.
distractors.B: Processing raw materials is manufacturing — only one stage.
distractors.C: Final delivery is the last step, not the whole chain.

questions.stem: An instruction given to a commercial bank to make regular payments of a fixed amount to a named person is called a
questions.explanation: A standing order is a customer instruction to the bank to make regular fixed payments — for rent, school fees or insurance — on set dates.

distractors.A: A direct debit lets the payee pull variable amounts; the customer authorises the company, not the bank, with the amount.
distractors.B: A credit transfer is a one-off transfer, not a recurring instruction.
distractors.C: A letter of credit is a trade finance document, not a domestic payment instruction.

questions.stem: One of the MAJOR functions of a central bank is to
questions.explanation: Only the central bank has the authority to issue notes and coins — its core function as the bankers' bank and currency authority.

distractors.B: Stocks and shares are issued by companies on the stock exchange, not the central bank.
distractors.C: Accepting deposit accounts is a commercial bank function.
distractors.D: Lending to the public is also a commercial bank function.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a source of income for an individual who is engaged in personal budgeting?
questions.explanation: Personal income comes in from salaries, interest and dividends. A loan repayment is money going out, so it is not a source of income.

distractors.A: Salaries are the most common source of household income.
distractors.C: Interest from savings adds to income each period.
distractors.D: Dividends from shares are investment income.

questions.stem: Financial investments can be defined as

options.B: stock of goods available in a country
options.C: amount of money available at a given time
options.D: amount of capital owned by the government

questions.explanation: Financial investment means buying assets that generate income — shares, bonds, deposits and similar instruments that pay dividends or interest.

distractors.B: The stock of goods is inventory, not financial investment.
distractors.C: Money on hand is liquid cash, not investment.
distractors.D: Government-owned capital is one form of public assets, not the definition.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an economic aim of government?
questions.explanation: Stabilising the national currency is squarely an economic aim of government — keeping inflation and exchange rates under control. Literacy, security and the legal system are social and political aims.

distractors.A: Raising literacy is a social aim.
distractors.C: Developing the legal system is a political/social aim.
distractors.D: Maintaining a security force is a defence/social aim.

questions.stem: Subsidies refer to
questions.explanation: Subsidies are grants given by government to producers to lower their costs and keep prices affordable — for example, fuel or farming subsidies in many Caribbean states.

distractors.B: Withholding taxes is the opposite — taking money in, not giving it out.
distractors.C: Loans must be repaid; subsidies do not.
distractors.D: Investment profits are returns, not government grants.

questions.stem: The success of a government's economic policies can be MOST effectively measured by

options.A: an increase in the level of policies
options.B: an increase in the standard of living
options.C: a decrease in the level of employment
options.D: a decrease in the volume of production

questions.explanation: The clearest measure of a successful economic policy is a rising standard of living — citizens can afford more goods, services and a better quality of life.

distractors.A: Issuing more policies isn't the same as those policies working.
distractors.C: Falling employment would be a sign of policy failure, not success.
distractors.D: Lower production usually signals economic decline.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a negative consequence of industrialization?

options.A: Increased unemployment in the manufacturing sector

questions.explanation: Industrialisation expands manufacturing but typically increases industrial waste — air, water and solid pollution are well-known negative side-effects in countries like Trinidad and Jamaica.

distractors.A: Industrialisation usually increases manufacturing-sector employment, not unemployment.
distractors.B: More industrial activity raises tax revenue, not lowers it.
distractors.D: Industrialisation increases the volume of goods produced.
